title: Why Matter Has Structure: Pauli as a Fact About Determinants

metadata.color_law: TEAL = distinct orbitals (different columns, nonzero determinant, allowed state); CRIMSON = identical orbitals (duplicate columns, determinant collapses to zero, forbidden)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no N-particle expansion, no Hartree-Fock detour, no periodic-table filling rules
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Two electrons cannot occupy the same spin-orbital. In a Slater determinant, trying to duplicate one spin-orbital makes two columns identical, and the many-electron wave function collapses to exactly zero.

props.command: Why does duplicating one fermion spin-orbital make a Slater determinant vanish?
props.segment: Why Matter Has Structure: Pauli as a Fact About Determinants

card.title: Why Matter Has Structure: Pauli as a Fact About Determinants

beats.act: COLD OPEN — the Pauli rule as stated
beats.narration_text: Pauli formulated the exclusion principle in 1925, before modern spin language was complete. His 1945 Nobel citation recognized the principle. Today we express the rule through fermionic antisymmetry — but that antisymmetry is the physical input, not something ordinary determinant algebra proves from nothing.

props.headline: Pauli stated in 1925 that no two electrons can share the…

beats.act: COLD OPEN — Slater saw it as a determinant
beats.narration_text: Slater introduced determinant wave functions in 1929. Put particle coordinates in rows and occupied spin-orbitals in columns. Exchanging two particle coordinates swaps two rows and flips the sign automatically. Once fermionic antisymmetry is required, the determinant packages it cleanly.

props.headline: In 1929, Slater showed it was a theorem. Write the two-electron…

beats.narration_text: Two electrons occupy distinct spin-orbitals, so the determinant can be nonzero. Now duplicate one complete spin-orbital — spatial shape and spin label together. Why must the determinant vanish, and why does sharing only the same spatial orbital not always violate Pauli?

props.headline: Two electrons are in different orbitals. The determinant is nonzero. Now…

card.title: Does a two-identical-column determinant really enforce the Pauli exclusion principle?

beats.act: THE PROBLEM — antisymmetry is the real requirement
beats.narration_text: The fermion requirement is antisymmetry: swap two electrons and the wave function changes sign. A determinant satisfies that automatically because swapping two rows flips its sign. Exclusion follows from antisymmetry, rather than being an additional independent rule inside this framework.

props.headline: The real requirement is antisymmetry: swap two electrons and the wave…

beats.narration_text: Take a two-by-two Slater determinant. Row one evaluates spin-orbitals at particle one; row two at particle two. Column one is chi A and column two chi B. If the occupied spin-orbitals are linearly independent, the antisymmetrized state need not vanish.

props.headline: Take a two-by-two Slater determinant. Row one: particle one. Row two:…

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — duplicate columns collapse to zero
beats.narration_text: Now set chi B equal to chi A. The two columns become identical. A determinant with identical columns is exactly zero, so the proposed many-electron state has zero norm and cannot represent a physical state. This is exclusion for duplicate spin-orbitals.

props.headline: Now make both orbitals identical. Column one and column two become…

beats.act: THE IMPLICATION — no chemistry without it
beats.narration_text: Exclusion is essential to shell filling and the periodic table, together with Coulomb attraction, electron repulsion, and quantum dynamics. Without fermionic occupation constraints, matter would have radically different structure. The determinant zero is the algebraic signature, not the whole Hamiltonian of chemistry.

props.headline: Without this constraint every electron in every atom would collapse to…

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Helium has two electrons in the same 1s spatial orbital. Is that forbidden? No: one occupies 1s alpha and the other 1s beta, two orthogonal spin-orbitals. But trying to place both in 1s alpha duplicates a determinant column, giving zero everywhere.

props.headline: A student writes the wave function for helium with both electrons…

beats.narration_text: Why does Pauli appear as a determinant zero? Fermionic antisymmetry is the physical requirement. A Slater determinant implements it. Duplicate a complete spin-orbital and two columns coincide, forcing the wave function to vanish. Same spatial orbital with opposite spins remains allowed.

props.headline: Pauli's exclusion principle is not a separate postulate. An antisymmetric multi-electron…

card.title: Pauli exclusion is a theorem: identical columns collapse the determinant to zero.
